skill.name: saga-orchestration
skill.description: Implement saga patterns for distributed transactions and cross-aggregate workflows. Use when coordinating multi-step business processes, handling compensating transactions, or managing long-running operations across microservices.
skill.summary: Implement saga patterns for distributed transactions and cross-aggregate workflows. Use when coordinating multi-step business processes, handling compensating transactions, or managing long-running operations.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 19 static findings are false positives. The pattern-matching scanner misidentified Python code templates as Ruby shell execution, markdown text as cryptographic algorithms, and service names in diagrams as C2 indicators. The skill contains legitimate saga orchestration patterns for distributed systems with no security risks.

medium_findings.title: Pattern Matching False Positives
medium_findings.description: Scanner flagged Python code blocks as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. The code uses legitimate Python syntax (import, dataclass, async/await) for saga templates.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Python code blocks clearly visible - uses 'async def', 'import', 'dataclass' which are Python syntax, not Ruby backticks

medium_findings.title: Misidentified Markdown Content
medium_findings.description: Scanner flagged text patterns as 'weak cryptographic algorithm' and 'C2 keywords'. These are normal text: best practices, table headers, and service names in ASCII diagrams.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Content includes legitimate technical terms: 'timeout policy', table headers, UUID import, and service placeholders (Svc1, Svc2, Svc3)

medium_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs - Legitimate References
medium_findings.description: Two URLs referenced in the resources section are legitimate technical references: microservices.io pattern documentation and a published book.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: URLs point to known technical resources (microservices.io patterns, dataintensive.net book) - standard educational references

content.user_title: Implement Saga Patterns for Distributed Transactions
content.value_statement: Coordinate multi-step business processes across microservices with reliable compensation handling when failures occur.
actual_capabilities: Implement orchestration-based saga patterns with centralized coordinators
Implement choreography-based saga patterns using event-driven architecture
Design and execute compensating transactions for failure recovery
Handle step timeouts and failure escalation in long-running workflows
Create idempotent saga steps for safe retry handling
Build order fulfillment and approval workflows across services
limitations: Does not provide runtime execution environment or message infrastructure
Does not include database or persistence layer implementations
Requires integration with existing event publishing systems
Does not handle distributed saga coordination across process boundaries

use_cases.title: E-commerce Order Processing
use_cases.description: Implement multi-step order fulfillment with inventory reservation, payment processing, and shipping orchestration with automatic rollback on failures.

use_cases.title: Financial Transaction workflows
use_cases.description: Create reliable financial processes with atomic compensation for multi-step transfers, refunds, and reconciliation tasks.

use_cases.title: Cross-Service Approval Systems
use_cases.description: Build approval workflows that coordinate across multiple services with automatic compensation if approvals are revoked or expire.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Saga Structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Python saga orchestrator base class that manages saga state (Started, Pending, Compensating, Completed, Failed) with methods for starting a saga, handling step completion, and executing compensation in reverse order on failure.
prompt_templates.scenario: Need a foundation for implementing saga patterns in a Python microservices application

prompt_templates.title: Order Fulfillment Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Implement an OrderFulfillmentSaga that coordinates reserve_inventory, process_payment, create_shipment, and send_confirmation steps with appropriate compensation actions for each step.
prompt_templates.scenario: Building a complete order processing pipeline with rollback capability

prompt_templates.title: Choreography-Based Events
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an event-driven choreography saga that uses an event bus to publish and subscribe to events like OrderCreated, InventoryReserved, PaymentProcessed, and ShipmentCreated without a central orchestrator.
prompt_templates.scenario: Implementing decentralized saga coordination using event-driven architecture

prompt_templates.title: Saga with Timeout Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Extend the saga orchestrator to include timeout handling for each step. Add scheduled timeout checks that trigger compensation if a step does not complete within the configured time window.
prompt_templates.scenario: Adding reliability to long-running saga steps that may hang or fail silently

output_examples.input: Create a saga orchestrator for order processing with payment and inventory steps
output_examples.output: A Python class implementing SagaOrchestrator with define_steps method returning SagaStep objects, async methods for start, handle_step_completed, and handle_step_failed with proper state transitions

output_examples.input: Show how to handle compensation for a failed payment in an order saga
output_examples.output: Compensation logic that iterates through completed steps in reverse order, publishing compensation events like RefundPayment and ReleaseInventory to undo the effects of successful steps

best_practices: Always design compensation actions before implementing forward actions - compensation must be reliable
Use idempotency keys for all commands to safely handle duplicate messages without side effects
Store correlation IDs across all events and logs to trace saga execution across service boundaries
anti_patterns: Do not assume saga steps complete instantly - always implement async handling and state persistence
Do not skip compensation testing - simulate failure at every step and verify rollback works
Do not couple services directly - use async event messaging to maintain loose coupling

faq.question: What is the difference between orchestration and choreography saga patterns?
faq.answer: Orchestration uses a central coordinator that directs all steps. Choreography uses events where each service reacts to events from other services. Orchestration provides better visibility and control; choreography offers lower coupling.

faq.question: How do compensations work in saga patterns?
faq.answer: Compensations are undo actions that reverse the effects of completed steps. They run in reverse order when a step fails. For example, if payment succeeds but shipping fails, the compensation would refund the payment.

faq.question: What happens if a compensation also fails?
faq.answer: The saga enters a Failed state after compensation completes. Operators should be notified for manual intervention. Design compensations to be idempotent and reliable to minimize this scenario.

faq.question: How do timeouts work in saga patterns?
faq.answer: Timeouts are tracked per step. If a step does not complete within the timeout window, the saga treats it as a failure and triggers compensation. Use a scheduler to check for timed-out steps.

faq.question: Can saga patterns handle duplicate messages?
faq.answer: Yes, by using idempotency keys. Each command includes a unique key, and handlers check if the key was already processed before executing the action. This prevents double-charging or double-shipping.

faq.question: When should I not use saga patterns?
faq.answer: Avoid sagas when you need strong consistency (ACID transactions), when operations are very short and can use local transactions, or when the business process is simple enough for a single service.
